chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator is mp4_write_one_h264 in libTPMp4Encoder.so; scudo deallocate invalid chunk state indicates heap-use-after-free or double-free in app code, not just OOM.

Without direct disassembly (tools 404), I infer mp4_write_one_h264 manages H.264 NAL units and MP4 muxing; incorrect lifetime or double-free on frame buffers can trigger scudo::reportInvalidChunkState during free.

The crash occurs during mp4_write_one_h264 called from JNI Java_com_tplink_skylight_common_jni_MP4Encoder_packVideo, which suggests Java supplies encoded frame data/length and timestamps; fuzz harness likely feeds arbitrary byte arrays in place of real frames.

Even if fuzzer passes unrealistic inputs, a well-written encoder must validate lengths and ownership before freeing; scudo invalid-chunk implies internal bookkeeping bug (double free / freeing unallocated / corrupted metadata) reachable via those inputs.

Because packVideo is a real JNI used by the app’s MP4 encoder pipeline (used for recording/export), a remote or local attacker controlling video stream or file contents could reach this path under realistic operation.

However, we lack decompiled code to see exact pointer/length origin (could be a pure logic lifetime bug independent of attacker size control). Still, any double free or heap UAF is a memory safety vulnerability independent of precise input control.

Given the evidence (allocator abort, deallocate in mp4_write_one_h264, production encoder lib, JNI entry used in app), I classify this as likely genuine heap memory corruption (CWE-415/416) with potential for exploitation but guarded by hardened allocator abort (scudo), so impact high but exploitation complexity non-trivial.

Cannot construct an exact Java callgraph chain, but AndroidManifest and class names show recording/encoder features; attack surface plausible via user-supplied/remote camera media processed into MP4 through this JNI.
reasons: scudo::reportInvalidChunkState during deallocate strongly indicates heap-use-after-free or double-free rather than benign abort
Failing function mp4_write_one_h264 is application encoder code in libTPMp4Encoder.so, not the harness or sanitizer itself
Function is invoked via real JNI bridge Java_com_tplink_skylight_common_jni_MP4Encoder_packVideo used by the app’s MP4 encoder pipeline, so reachable with attacker-influenced media data
Memory lifetime bug in encoder can be triggered by crafted sequence/structure of H.264 frames, leading to heap corruption before scudo aborts
recommendations: Manually reverse-engineer mp4_write_one_h264 and surrounding helpers to locate all malloc/free ownership pairs and identify double free / UAF patterns
Add allocator wrappers or debug logs around allocations used by mp4_write_one_h264 to record pointer ownership and reference counts during fuzzing
Introduce stricter validation of frame buffer lifetimes and ensure each allocation is freed exactly once, avoiding reuse after free; add regression tests with fuzzing focused on MP4Encoder.packVideo
assumptions: MP4Encoder.packVideo is invoked in normal app flows for video recording or file export
Input to packVideo (H.264 frames / buffers) can be influenced by remote sources (camera stream, uploaded files) at least indirectly
The scudo invalid chunk state is caused by app code misuse (double free/UAF) rather than external memory corruption from unrelated components
limitations: No direct decompiled view of mp4_write_one_h264 or JNI glue, so pointer provenance and exact bug pattern are inferred
Java-side parameter handling for MP4Encoder.packVideo could not be inspected in detail; exact attacker control over lengths and buffers is unknown
Stack trace does not show whether other threads are sharing the same buffers, so concurrent lifetime issues are only speculative

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying crafted or malformed H.264 frame sequences into the app’s MP4 encoder pipeline that reach MP4Encoder.packVideo
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the video data being recorded or processed by the app (e.g., remote camera stream, uploaded clip, or local file)
Target device runs vulnerable version of com.tplink.skylight with libTPMp4Encoder.so
Scudo or similar hardened allocator is present, which may convert corruption into abort instead of silent control-flow hijack
exploit_pipeline: Prepare a specially crafted H.264 bitstream or frame sequence designed to stress buffer reuse and edge cases (e.g., zero-length or extremely large NAL units, unusual SPS/PPS ordering)

Deliver this bitstream to the app in a way that forces it to invoke MP4Encoder.packVideo repeatedly (e.g., by streaming from a compromised camera or feeding a file into a feature that transcodes/records)
Trigger the encoder to process enough frames so that mp4_write_one_h264 reaches the buggy free path, causing double free / heap corruption and allocator abort
Iterate payload tuning under a debugger or with ASAN-style builds of libTPMp4Encoder to attempt moving from deterministic abort to controlled heap layout for possible RCE on less-hardened builds
poc_commands: adb shell "am start -n com.tplink.skylight/.feature.mainTab.memories.MemoriesVideoPlayActivity --es input /sdcard/Download/payload.h264" # placeholder, depends on actual entry for encoding
exploit.notes: Exact UI or service entry that calls MP4Encoder.packVideo is not determined; PoC assumes there is a recording or export path that invokes this JNI with H.264 frames derived from attacker-controlled media.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame below allocator/sanitizer is mp4_write_one_jpeg in libTPMp4Encoder.so, with abort coming from scudo invalid chunk state -> deallocate, indicating a heap misuse such as double free or invalid free inside encoder code.

We cannot decompile the native library via tools due to environment limitations (404/analyze_new_binary not available), but the stack shows scudo detecting invalid deallocation during mp4_write_one_jpeg, not in the fuzz harness itself. This strongly suggests the encoder's internal memory management is incorrect for some input patterns.

Crash path: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_tplink_skylight_common_jni_MP4Encoder_packVideo -> mp4_write_one_jpeg -> scudo::Allocator::deallocate -> abort. The JNI method is a real app method in com.tplink.skylight.common.jni.MP4Encoder, used by MP4Encoder Java class and derived encoders. It is likely reachable in production whenever the app packs video frames into MP4.

Fuzzer supplies arbitrary data via fuzz_one_input into the JNI bridge. In production, inputs typically come from camera or local data; although not directly remote, malformed frames or crafted local content (e.g. imported JPEG frames) could reach packVideo. Even if typical usage is camera-only, many mobile cameras use external encoders, and feed raw YUV rather than arbitrary data. However, we can't prove the app constrains or validates input sizes/pointers fully, especially around JPEG frame handling.

The nature of the abort (invalid chunk state during free) means that for some sequence of calls or data, mp4_write_one_jpeg triggers heap corruption / double free. That is a classic memory safety issue (CWE-415/416) and potentially exploitable for RCE, but we lack details on control over heap contents and attacker controllability of structure fields. Given a general encoder processing attacker-provided media, such bugs are typically high severity.

Because the harness entry is fuzz_one_input and the JNI method matches the real app bridge, the crash is unlikely to be a pure harness artifact like passing NULL env or impossible object shapes. Instead, it's caused by library logic incorrectly managing allocations - a real vulnerability, albeit with some uncertainty about practical exploit surface (is input attacker-controlled or just local camera frames?). We'll classify as vulnerable with medium-high confidence and high severity due to heap misuse in a media parser/encoder.

We note missing pieces: we couldn't inspect mp4_write_one_jpeg implementation, nor the exact Java packVideo signature to see parameter validation. Therefore we can't model exact exploit steps, but we can outline a generic pipeline: crafted video/JPEG frames passed to packVideo from an attacker-controlled source (e.g., network stream, shared file) that causes inconsistent allocation/free paths, leading to heap corruption and eventually abort or code execution.

Given this, we mark is_vulnerable=true, confidence ~0.7, severity=high, CWE-415/416, and outline generic recommendations: audit mp4_write_one_jpeg and adjacent allocation paths, apply strict lifetime ownership, add fuzzing with ASan/scudo (already showing issue), and ensure Java layer constrains frame sizes and counts.
reasons: Abort originates from scudo invalid chunk state during deallocate, indicating heap misuse (double free/invalid free).

The faulting function mp4_write_one_jpeg is application code in libTPMp4Encoder.so, called directly from the JNI bridge Java_com_tplink_skylight_common_jni_MP4Encoder_packVideo, not from the fuzz harness itself.
JNI bridge corresponds to a real app class (com.tplink.skylight.common.jni.MP4Encoder), meaning the buggy path is part of production functionality.
Such heap misuse in a media encoder can typically be triggered via crafted or unexpected frame data and is potentially exploitable beyond a simple crash.
recommendations: Manually audit mp4_write_one_jpeg and related allocation/free logic for double frees, mismatched allocators, and use-after-free; add assertions for ownership and lifetime.
Augment fuzzing of MP4Encoder.packVideo with varied frame sizes, counts, and malformed JPEG data under sanitizer builds to reproduce and minimize the failing input.
Introduce defensive checks in the JNI and Java layers to validate buffer sizes, frame counts, and pointer lifetimes before calling into native encoding routines.
assumptions: MP4Encoder.packVideo is reachable in normal app use and processes data that could be influenced by untrusted or semi-trusted sources (e.g., files, streams).
The scudo invalid chunk state is caused by a logic bug in mp4_write_one_jpeg, not by deliberate harness misuse of allocation APIs.
The fuzz harness passes inputs that are structurally similar to what the real app might pass, at least enough to reach the same code paths.
limitations: Native library libTPMp4Encoder.so could not be decompiled in this environment, so the exact faulty code pattern (double free vs. use-after-free) is unknown.

Java-side implementation of com.tplink.skylight.common.jni.MP4Encoder.packVideo and upstream call graph were not inspected in detail here, limiting understanding of actual attacker control over input.
No minimized crashing test case or register/memory dump at crash time was available to verify heap layout and practical exploitability.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying crafted or unexpected JPEG/video frame data to MP4Encoder.packVideo via a feature that records or encodes media.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process attacker-controlled or corrupted media data through the MP4 encoding pipeline (e.g., via imported file, network stream, or shared content).
Vulnerable libTPMp4Encoder.so is present and used by the running app version.
ASLR and other mitigations are present, so reliable exploitation would require heap grooming and knowledge of allocator behavior.
exploit_pipeline: Identify an app feature that invokes com.tplink.skylight.common.jni.MP4Encoder.packVideo (e.g., a recording, export, or share-to-MP4 function).

Craft a media input (sequence of JPEG frames or video data) that mimics the structure exercised by the fuzzer and triggers inconsistent allocation/free paths in mp4_write_one_jpeg, reproducing the scudo invalid free.
Use iterative fuzzing/mutation targeted at the encoder inputs to stabilize a crash where heap metadata and adjacent chunks are controllably corrupted rather than just aborting immediately.
Leverage the heap corruption primitives (e.g., overlapping chunks, tcache poisoning) to overwrite function pointers or vtables used later in the encoding pipeline and redirect control flow.
Deploy the crafted media to a target device and have the victim app open/process it, resulting in a crash or potential arbitrary code execution in the app process.
exploit.notes: Exact triggering interface (activity name, extras) is unknown; adapt the PoC command to the specific activity or service that initiates MP4 encoding in this app.
